column2: A physical or virtual location where healthcare is delivered, such as a hospital, clinic, home, or telehealth visit; different settings match different levels of patient acuity.
column4: Choosing the right care setting — urgent care versus the emergency department — is often the first decision a patient or family makes.

column2: How sick or medically unstable a patient is at a given moment; higher acuity calls for more intensive settings and staff.
column4: A patient with chest pain has higher acuity than a patient with a sprained ankle, so the two belong in different care settings.

column2: First-contact, continuing care that handles common problems, prevention, and coordination with specialists; usually delivered in an outpatient clinic.
column4: A family physician, pediatrician, or nurse practitioner in primary care is often the hub that refers patients into and out of other settings.

column2: Short-term, intensive treatment for severe illness or injury, typically delivered in a hospital's emergency department or inpatient units.
column4: A patient admitted for a heart attack receives acute care in the hospital before stepping down to rehabilitation or home.

column2: Care that follows a hospital stay to help a patient recover, including skilled nursing facilities, inpatient rehabilitation, and home health services.
column4: After a hip replacement, many patients move to post-acute care for a few weeks of physical therapy before returning home.

column2: The delivery of clinical services — visits, consultations, monitoring — using video, phone, or secure messaging instead of an in-person visit.
column4: Telehealth expanded rapidly during the 2020s and is now a routine setting for follow-up visits and behavioral health.

column2: The organization that pays the bill for a healthcare service — typically a private insurer or a public program such as Medicare or Medicaid — distinct from the provider who delivers the care.
column4: A patient may see a provider in one network while the claim is processed by a different payer, which is why the payer-provider distinction matters.

column2: A federal health-insurance program primarily for people age 65 and older and certain people with disabilities, administered by CMS.
column4: Medicare is the payer for most Americans once they turn 65, even if the hospital that treats them is private.

column2: A joint federal-state health-coverage program for people with low income and certain other eligible groups; each state runs its own program within federal rules.
column4: Medicaid covers a large share of children, pregnant patients, and long-term nursing-home residents in the United States.

column2: Children's Health Insurance Program — a joint federal-state program covering children in families whose income is too high for Medicaid but who otherwise lack affordable coverage.
column4: CHIP fills the gap for children whose families earn above the Medicaid threshold but cannot afford private premiums.

column2: The full sequence of services a patient may use over time, from prevention and primary care through acute, specialty, post-acute, and end-of-life care.
column4: Health-system design is mostly about keeping the continuum of care connected so patients don't fall through the gaps.

column2: The hand-off when a patient moves between settings or providers — for example, hospital to home, or primary care to specialist; a common source of errors and readmissions.
column4: Medication lists, discharge instructions, and follow-up appointments are the fragile parts of every care transition.

column2: Any factor that keeps a person from getting needed healthcare — cost, distance, language, insurance status, transportation, or digital access — and that shapes outcomes independent of clinical need.
column4: A rural patient with diabetes may face two access barriers at once: distance to a clinic and limited home internet for telehealth.
